factual-accuracy.requirement: States individualism scores: South Korea collectivist (IDV 18±5), Finland individualist (IDV 63±5), Mexico collectivist (IDV 30±5)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states South Korea’s individualism as approximately 18 and Mexico’s as approximately 30, but it does not present Finland’s individualism score as 63±5. It also does not explicitly frame the three as “collectivist/individualist” with the requested IDV ranges for all three countries.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States power distance scores: South Korea mid-high (PDI 60±10), Finland low (PDI 33±5), Mexico very high (PDI 81±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states South Korea’s PDI as approximately 60, Finland’s PDI as 33, and Mexico’s PDI as approximately 81, matching the required mid-high/low/very high ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States uncertainty avoidance: South Korea very high (UAI 85±10), Finland moderate (UAI 59±10), Mexico high (UAI 82±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides power distance and individualism scores for South Korea, Finland, and Mexico, but it never states uncertainty avoidance (UAI) values for any of the three countries. It does not mention South Korea very high UAI (~85), Finland moderate UAI (~59), or Mexico high UAI (~82) within the specified ±10 ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Classting as teacher-moderated or teacher-led class SNS with private counseling rooms/secret channels and coarse moderation controls
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Classting as a teacher-controlled class SNS, noting only teachers can create classes and that teachers have granular posting permissions and real-time monitoring/moderation. It also mentions “Private Counseling” as a one-on-one private channel (optionally anonymous), which functions like private counseling rooms/secret channels.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Wilma has 95-98% coverage in Finnish basic education schools and handles attendance/grades/messaging
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states Wilma is used in “more than 95% of primary and secondary schools” in Finland and describes it as handling communication/messaging plus grades/assessments and attendance/absences/behavioral records. This satisfies the required coverage range (95–98%) and the functions (attendance, grades, messaging).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Google Classroom Mexico/Latin America deployments reached state scale (hundreds of thousands to 1M+ students) with private comments and teacher-controlled posting emphasized
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Google Classroom deployments in Mexico reached state-scale adoption (e.g., “1.3 million students in Jalisco”) and explicitly describes “private comments” as teacher-student only, plus notes teachers can control whether students can post/comment on the Stream.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous peer assessment increases cognitive/higher-order feedback or improves final grades significantly versus identified review
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions a Thai EFL study where anonymous peer feedback via Google Classroom significantly improved the quality of peer feedback, but it does not state that anonymity increases cognitive/higher-order feedback or that it significantly improves final grades versus identified review. It also does not provide a direct comparison against identified peer review outcomes.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous question queues or LAQQ reduce apprehension and increase question submission versus public-only channels
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response repeatedly argues that anonymous/private channels are important in high power-distance, face-saving contexts (Korea, Mexico) because students are reluctant to ask questions or give critical feedback publicly, and it recommends implementing anonymous feedback options to increase participation compared with public class comments.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains that high power distance reduces willingness to publicly challenge peers or request help in digital learning environments
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response repeatedly links high power distance contexts (Korea, Mexico) to reluctance to participate openly, noting students may be reluctant to ask questions or give critical peer feedback publicly and recommending private/anonymous channels to avoid face-loss. It also contrasts low power distance Finland as more comfortable with direct, identified feedback.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends masked/anonymous early drafts, teacher-approved public showcases, anonymous live Q&A, or teacher-moderated boards for Korean group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends anonymity and teacher moderation for Korea, including “anonymous peer feedback,” “anonymous private messaging,” and “teacher approves all posts,” and also suggests “opt-in public sharing for positive exemplars only,” which aligns with teacher-approved public showcases.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named edits/contributions, visible cursors, open collaboration, or student self-organization for Finnish group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Finland-specific collaboration features such as “identified, direct peer feedback (optional)” and “student autonomy,” including giving students greater control over feedback processes. However, it does not explicitly recommend named edits/contributions, visible cursors, open collaboration, or student self-organization for Finnish group workspaces.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends private-first design, mobile/offline editing, teacher-controlled visibility, or teacher-only posting initially for Mexican group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Mexico-specific design choices including a “teacher-first deployment,” “visible teacher authority,” defaulting to “private correction,” and adding “offline-capable feedback mechanisms” plus an “anonymous feedback toggle” for peer assessment.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends double-blind or anonymous peer review with rubric-guided stems or structured feedback prompts for Korea
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends anonymous feedback for South Korea and also proposes structured peer feedback templates/prompts (e.g., “structured peer feedback templates with praise-first framing” and “peer feedback templates with praise-first framing”) as design priorities for Korea.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named peer review with feedback literacy scaffolding, exemplars, or process-focused critique for Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Finland-specific peer feedback approaches, stating that peer feedback could be identified and optional, and it also proposes process scaffolds such as structured peer feedback templates, exemplars (e.g., “opt-in public sharing for positive exemplars only”), and formative/process-focused critique via growth-oriented tracking.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends two-tier identity (anonymous to peers, identified to teacher) or private-first peer review with structured stems for Mexico
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends for Mexico a “native anonymous feedback toggle for peer assessment” and also suggests integrating Google Forms/third-party tools for anonymous peer assessment, which aligns with a private-first or two-tier identity approach. However, it does not specifically recommend the two-tier identity model (anonymous to peers but identified to teacher) nor does it clearly propose structured peer-review stems for Mexico.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Claims Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, or claims Mexico has lower power distance than Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states Finland’s power distance is 33, South Korea’s is ~60, and Mexico’s is ~81, which implies Finland is lower than both Korea and Mexico. It does not claim Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, nor that Mexico has lower power distance than Finland.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Predicts anonymity or power-distance interventions will show stronger effects in high-PDI markets (Korea/Mexico) versus low-PDI (Finland)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that anonymous feedback is essential in high power distance contexts (Korea, Mexico) and less critical in low power distance Finland, and recommends anonymous feedback defaults/toggles for Korea and Mexico.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Describes temporal rollout strategies (private-first then gradual opening) with triggers or norms that enable stage transitions
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response describes staged, time-ordered rollout norms such as Google Classroom’s “teacher-first” deployment (training and devices to teachers before scaling to students) and implies stage transitions triggered by teacher readiness/adoption. It also recommends configurable moderation/anonymity presets with culturally determined defaults, which functions as a gradual opening strategy.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies negative consequences of recommended patterns (e.g., fairness concerns) and proposes compensating design elements or safeguards
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response flags multiple downsides of the recommended patterns (e.g., Finland’s “surveillance”/permanent behavioral records as a “criminal record,” Mexico’s digital divide equity issues, and lack of anonymity in high power-distance contexts) and then proposes safeguards such as flexible anonymity toggles, configurable moderation presets, formative/updateable error records, and offline/multi-channel feedback options.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies measurable outcomes beyond raw counts (higher-order comments, revision adoption rates) with expected effect ranges from research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides mostly raw scale/adoption metrics (users, schools, messages, satisfaction) and some learning-level percentages, but it does not specify higher-order measurable outcomes like higher-quality comments or revision adoption rates. It also does not give expected effect-size ranges from research for those outcomes. The required beyond-count outcomes and effect ranges are missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects technical constraints (offline capability, bandwidth limits) to required collaboration design patterns in specific markets
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response briefly mentions Mexico’s digital divide and suggests “offline-capable feedback mechanisms” and multi-channel communication, but it does not connect offline capability or bandwidth limits to concrete real-time collaboration/workspace design patterns for South Korea, Finland, and Mexico. There is no market-by-market mapping from technical constraints to specific collaboration patterns (e.g., async-first, low-bandwidth modes, conflict resolution/merge patterns).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Draws explicit connections between existing platform capabilities (private channels, moderation toggles) and cultural requirements they address
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly links platform features to cultural needs, e.g., Classting’s “Private Counseling” anonymity to Korean face-saving, teacher-only class creation/posting permissions to high power distance, and Google Classroom’s Stream posting/comment controls to Mexico’s teacher-centric norms. It also contrasts Wilma’s lack of anonymous/peer channels with Finnish low power distance and directness.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes contexts where cultural predictions don't hold or explicitly flags inferences versus direct empirical evidence
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly distinguishes platform-specific evidence (e.g., “no native anonymous feedback” in Google Classroom; Wilma lacks peer messaging) from broader cultural interpretations, and it notes when claims are based on global platform limitations or external studies rather than local deployments. It also flags uncertainty/absence of evidence explicitly (e.g., “No evidence exists that Wilma supports anonymous feedback”).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides identical design recommendations for all three markets without adapting interaction patterns to their different PDI or collectivism profiles
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response differentiates recommendations by market (e.g., Korea: anonymous/private feedback and high teacher control; Finland: identified/direct feedback and lower-default moderation; Mexico: teacher-first deployment and private correction with added anonymity needs). It does not give identical design recommendations across all three contexts.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis by feature or market with consistent comparative structure (e.g., Korea/Finland/Mexico breakdowns per feature)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is consistently organized by market/platform (Classting/Korea, Wilma/Finland, Google Classroom/Mexico) and repeats the same comparative feature headings (anonymous vs identified feedback, teacher moderation, public vs private mistake visibility, outcome metrics), including cross-market comparison tables.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise UX/product design terminology (interaction patterns, moderation controls, engagement metrics, feature toggles)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses precise UX/product design terminology throughout, including interaction patterns (anonymous/private comments, class comments), moderation controls (posting permissions, approve/flag presets, AI-assisted moderation), engagement metrics (satisfaction %, messages/week, adoption counts), and feature toggles/presets (anonymity toggle, configurable moderation levels).

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective, research-oriented tone connecting design recommendations to empirical findings without marketing claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, analytical tone and repeatedly ties interface recommendations (anonymity, moderation, mistake visibility) to cited cultural-dimension research and platform behaviors/metrics. It avoids overt promotional language, generally presenting adoption and satisfaction figures as findings rather than marketing claims.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents engagement effects with specific metrics or ranges (e.g., percentage increases, effect sizes) rather than only qualitative descriptions
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple quantitative engagement-related metrics and ranges (e.g., 80%+ student satisfaction, 71.4% improvement, 300,000+ messages weekly, 65.5% regular use, 96.7% meaningful learning, and a T-test with p-value), rather than relying only on qualitative descriptions.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same cultural insight or design recommendation multiple times using different wording without adding new analysis
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly restates the same recommendations—e.g., anonymity for Korea/Mexico, identified feedback for Finland, high teacher moderation for Korea/Mexico, and private mistake correction—across the executive summary, cross-cultural analysis tables, and the later “Design Recommendations” sections with only minor rewording. These repetitions add little new analysis beyond earlier points.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Hofstede cultural dimensions framework for individualism and power distance scores across the three countries
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Hofstede’s framework and provides approximate Hofstede scores for both Power Distance Index and Individualism for South Korea (PDI ~60, IDV ~18), Finland (PDI 33, IDV ~63), and Mexico (PDI ~81, IDV ~30).

citation-quality.requirement: References official documentation or deployment reports for all 3 platforms: Classting, Wilma, and Google Classroom deployments
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes citations to official Classting support documentation, the official Wilma.fi site and related Wilma studies, and multiple Google Classroom official Help/Community pages plus Google for Education customer stories for Latin America deployments.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 academic sources on anonymous peer assessment, peer review, or participation mechanisms in educational contexts
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple academic sources related to peer assessment/peer feedback mechanisms, including a 2025 journal study on AI-based formative peer assessment in Korea and a 2019 study on anonymous online peer feedback via Google Classroom in a Thai EFL writing classroom.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes K-12 or secondary education research on cultural differences, engagement patterns, or digital collaboration
citation-quality.explanation: The response draws on secondary/K-12 contexts throughout (e.g., Korean middle/high schools, Wilma in Finnish primary and secondary schools, and Mexican public secondary school deployments) and discusses cultural differences affecting peer feedback, teacher authority, and public mistake-making in digital collaboration.

citation-quality.requirement: Uses Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not use Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness. While it cites a Google Classroom Community thread and a blog/YouTube link, these are not treated as the main evidentiary basis for the core claims or metrics.
